title: Trump Expected to Address Nation on Iran Strikes
text:
President Trump is expected to address the nation on Saturday, according to two U.S. officials, after American and Israeli airstrikes began pounding Iran and Iranian missiles targeted U.S. bases in the Gulf in retaliation.
Trump is expected to speak about the campaign against Iran, which started early Saturday morning, the two officials said.

title: Iranians Flee Tehran as Strikes Hit Cities
text:
Many residents in Tehran are fleeing the city as they brace for the more airstrikes, with heavy traffic on major roads.
A retired oil executive in Iran said he was packing for his villa on the Caspian Sea, having readied two cars with full fuel tanks in preparation. “People are really scared,” he said. He said he saw missiles flying down before hearing explosions.

title: Trump Posts News Story Accusing Iran of Trying to Interfere in U.S. Elections
text:
President Trump posted a news story early Saturday on social media accusing Iran of trying to interfere to stop him winning the 2020 and 2024 U.S. presidential elections.
Trump posted the headline of the article, which ties Iran’s alleged attempts at election interference to the latest U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran.

title: Oil Prices Have Been Rising Over Fears of Conflict
text:
We won't know for sure how the attack is moving oil prices until Sunday evening New York time, when trading in Brent futures opens. The benchmark oil price has risen about 19% this year to just over $72 a barrel, mostly because traders thought a conflict in the Middle East could disrupt exports.
The doomsday scenario for supplies is that Iran seeks to close the Strait of Hormuz, the deep channel between Iran and Oman through which flows almost a fifth of all the petroleum the world consumes each day. Earlier this month, prices jumped when Iran said the strait would partially close for "security precautions."

title: U.S. Bases Across Gulf Targeted by Iran
text:
Tehran is unleashing its stockpile of missiles against all but one of the U.S. bases in the Gulf in retaliation for the U.S. and Israeli attack, according to senior Arab officials.
A U.S. official said the U.S. hadn’t faced such a breadth of simultaneous strikes against its overseas bases before, but said U.S. personnel were prepared for the attacks.

title: Tankers Divert From Strait of Hormuz
text:
Dozens of tankers are diverting in the Persian Gulf, seeking refuge in Qatar and the United Arab Emirates or staying out of the area altogether after the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran.
Brokers said a loaded supertaker chartered by Shell that was supposed to cross the Strait of Hormuz is now idle in the Arabian Gulf and another is now crossing the strait at full speed on its way to South Korea. Two more Shell-chartered supertankers that were supposed to cross are now idle near Iraq.

title: Israel Calls Up 70,000 Reservists
text:
Israel has called up around 70,000 reservist soldiers, according to an Israeli military official.
The reservists will be bolstering the air defenses and home-front command as well as being stationed along Israel’s borders in case of attacks from its neighbors, the official said.

title: Some Iranians Hope Strikes Can Lead to Regime’s Fall
text: As U.S. and Israeli strikes hit targets across Iran, some opponents of the regime inside the country expressed hope this could be the beginning of the end of the Islamic Republic.

title: ‘Trump Has Met the Moment,’ Says Sen. Lindsey Graham
text:
In his first interview since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, Sen. Lindsey Graham (R, S.C.) said that “President Trump has met the moment” and “put in motion the demise of the Iranian ayatollah.”
“This operation will be massive in scope and has as its goal the elimination of the regime as demanded by the people of Iran,” the staunch Trump ally said, adding that the president concluded Tehran couldn’t be trusted to make a nuclear deal. “It has been a dream of mine for so long that the ayatollah’s murderous regime would be replaced by the people who seek a better life for themselves and their country.”

title: Civilian Killed in Iranian Missile Strikes on U.A.E.
text:
DUBAI—A civilian was killed as the United Arab Emirates came under a barrage of Iranian ballistic missile strikes, according to Emirati state media.
The U.A.E. said it intercepted several missiles and that fallen debris was the cause of the civilian death.

title: In Video, Trump Doesn’t Make Case for Imminent Iranian Threat
text:
In his video address announcing the military operation against Iran, President Trump said the U.S. faced “imminent threats” from Iran but didn’t make the case for why. Instead, he read off a list of previous Iranian aggressions while mentioning his general concern with Tehran’s nuclear work, support for regional proxies and ballistic-missile program.
Iran’s missile development became a major part of the administration’s case for war in recent days, with Trump mentioning it during Tuesday’s State of the Union address. But public U.S. intelligence indicated Iran hadn’t made the decision to make an intercontinental ballistic missile. Experts, meanwhile, said Tehran still had many technological hurdles to overcome before making a weapon that could successfully reach the U.S., even if Iran had missiles capable of striking allies in Europe.

title: Iran’s National Security Council Says People Should Leave Tehran If Possible
text:
Iran’s Supreme National Security Council urged people to travel if possible to other “cities and centers” away from Tehran and the other places being attacked by Israel and the U.S.
It said the essential supply of goods has been prepared and that banks will continue to operate normally. It urged people to avoid crowded shopping centers. Schools and universities will be closed until further notice and government offices will operate at 50% capacity for now, the council said.

title: Israeli Hospitals Moving Underground
text:
Some Israeli hospitals are moving their operations and patients underground as the country faces Iranian retaliatory strikes on Saturday.
“We are in the process of moving all of our departments and services into protected areas,” Itai Pessach, deputy director general of Sheba Medical Center near Tel Aviv, said in a video statement, adding that medical services continue as usual.

title: Iran Says Tehran Will Strike Back at ‘Aggressors,’ Urges U.N. Response
text:
Iran’s Foreign Ministry warned the country would hit back against its “aggressors” with all its capabilities as it urged the United Nations and nonaligned and Islamic countries to rally behind it.
Stressing that the attacks came amid diplomatic efforts to solve the nuclear issue, during the holy month of Ramadan and on the eve of the Persian new-year celebration Nowruz, the ministry said Israel and the U.S. had “attacked a number of defense targets and infrastructure, as well as civilian facilities, in various cities across our country.”

title: Iranian Missile Strike on Qatar Intercepted, Doha Says
text: Missiles launched toward the Al Udeid U.S. air base in Qatar were intercepted, the Qatar Defense Ministry said. Iran targeted U.S. bases in the Middle East after it was struck by American and Israeli forces Saturday.

title: Israel Targeting Iranian Regime Leadership
text:
As the U.S. and Israel conduct joint strikes on Iran, the Israeli military is targeting Iran’s leadership, according to a U.S. official and a person briefed on the operation.
Other targets of the operation include Iran’s ballistic missile supplies, a stockpile which has long threatened Israel as well as U.S. bases in the Middle East, and intelligence targets, including the Iranian Ministry of Intelligence, according to the person briefed. A video verified by The Wall Street Journal showed a large plume of smoke in an area near the ministry in Tehran.

title: Trump Administration Reverses Course on Starting Regime-Change Wars
text:
Since the second Trump administration came to office, it promised not to oversee a regime-change war. “The War Department will not be distracted by democracy building interventionism, undefined wars, regime change,” Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said in December.
In November 2024, shortly before President Trump won the election, top adviser Stephen Miller posted on social media that “Trump said warmongering neocons love sending your kids to die for wars they would never fight themselves.”

title: U.S. Urges Americans in Middle East to Shelter in Place
text:
DUBAI—U.S. embassies in several Middle Eastern countries are directing American personnel and citizens to shelter in place.
American missions in Israel, Jordan, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar and Bahrain are recommending Americans find secure locations, maintain low profiles and ensure they have a supply of food, water and other essentials.

title: Which Iranian Nuclear Sites Might Attacks Target?
text:
Last summer, the U.S. and Israel struck and caused severe damage to Iran’s three main nuclear sites: Isfahan, Fordow and Natanz. They remain severely damaged, although Iran has been doing some work around them.
Isfahan still has some buildings standing. The sites are believed to hold Iran’s stockpile of enriched uranium, including 440 kilograms of near weapons-grade uranium, enough to fuel around 12 nuclear bombs.

title: Iran Says Judiciary Chief Unhurt in Strikes
text: Gholam-Hossein Mohseni Eje'i, Iran’s chief justice, is in “complete health and well-being,” says the judiciary’s news agency, Mizan. Iran’s hardline judiciary plays a key role in the repression of opponents. In recent days he warned student protesters against burning flags or chanting antigovernment slogans.

title: U.S. Navy Base in Bahrain Targeted
text: The U.S. Navy base in Bahrain was targeted by Iran, officials said. The base, known as Naval Support Activity Bahrain, is the Navy’s headquarters in the Middle East and enables the U.S. to lead a coalition of partners in the region that work together to deter Iran, counter terrorism and maintain freedom of navigation.

title: Airspace Over the Middle East Starts to Close
text: Iranian airspace emptied out on Saturday morning, after the U.S. strikes. Civilian planes, including between Dubai and Germany, rapidly rerouted their flights to avoid Iranian airspace. Qatar, Iraq, Bahrain, the United Arab Emritates and Israel also closed their airspace, according to flight-tracking website Flightradar24.

title: Gulf States Raise Crude Exports, Fearing Disruption
text:
Gulf states, home to multiple U.S. military bases, have increased their crude exports in recent weeks and drawn up contingency plans, fearing strikes on Iran would lead to disruptions in the oil markets.
Saudi Arabia, the world's largest oil exporter, has raised its security to the highest level as a precautionary measure and has already activated its emergency plan to raise its exports to their highest level in nearly three years in case markets see supply disruptions, Gulf OPEC officials said.

title: Netanyahu Urges Iranians to Rise Up
text:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said that Israel and the U.S. were attacking Iranian ballistic missile sites and targets linked to the Basij, a paramilitary group.
“For 47 years, the evil regime in Iran has called ‘Death to Israel,’ ‘Death to America.’ It trampled its citizens,” Netanyahu said. “This operation will continue as long as needed.”

title: Gulf States Heighten Security Around Shipping Lanes
text:
Gulf Arab states have raised security around alternative routes for crude and prepared parallel supply chains across the region in case of disruption, according to Gulf officials familiar with the matter. Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates have also made emergency rerouting plans in case of retaliatory strikes from Iran, they said.
Saudi Arabia’s East–West pipeline can carry up to five million barrels a day to the Red Sea, while the Abu Dhabi–Fujairah pipeline allows part of the UAE’s exports to bypass the Strait of Hormuz.

title: Analysis: Trump’s Call for Regime Change in Iran Carries Risks
text:
President Trump on Saturday outlined a set of far-reaching aims for the current U.S. military operation against Iran. He called on Iranians to topple the regime and take over their country after the extensive U.S. and Israeli bombing campaign ends.
Trump’s declaration of broad aims is likely to rattle U.S. allies in the region, including Gulf states and Turkey, who are worried about a wider regional war that could bring instability to their doorsteps.

title: Pentagon Names Iran Mission ‘Operation Epic Fury’
text:
The Defense Department posted on social media that the Iran mission’s name is “Operation Epic Fury.”
It was the first public statement from the U.S. military since the beginning of the U.S. and Israeli attack. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth shared the post, which was in all-caps with an American flag.

title: Israel Identifies Missile Launches From Iran
text: Missile sirens were heard in the center of the country, including the Tel Aviv area. A missile interception was also heard over Jerusalem. Israel has a sophisticated missile-defense system known as the Iron Dome.

title: Iran’s Revolutionary Guards Launch Missiles and Drones at Israel
text: Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said they had launched the first wave of extensive missile and drone attacks toward Israel, according to a statement by Tasnim news agency, which is close to the security services. Sirens were heard in Jerusalem and northern Israel on Saturday.

title: Hardline Iranian News Agency Posts Footage of Missiles Ready to Launch
text: An official Iranian news agency has posted a video of missiles ready to launch following U.S. strikes. “When we talk about pinpointing targets,” said the caption published by the Student News Network, which is close to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.

title: What Combat Power Does the U.S. Have in the Middle East Right Now?
text:
The U.S. began attacking Iran on Saturday following a massive buildup of combat power.
Two aircraft carriers, the USS Abraham Lincoln and the USS Gerald R. Ford, are within striking distance of Iran.
The carriers are loaded with jet fighters that can attack targets, as well as electronic-warfare planes that can jam enemy defenses.
Navy guided-missile destroyers are operating in the region. They can fire Tomahawk cruise missiles as well as defensive interceptors.
The Air Force has also moved dozens of jet fighters and support aircraft at U.S. overseas bases: Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan, Ovda Air Base in Israel and Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia, according to flight-tracking and open-source data.
These include F-35s, F-15s, F-22s, F-16s, E-3 Sentry Airborne Warning and Control System and E-11A Battlefield Airborne Communications Node aircraft.

title: ‘American Heroes May Be Lost’ During Iran Operation, Trump Says
text:
President Trump acknowledged that U.S. troops could be killed after launching a military operation against Iran over its nuclear work and ballistic missile program—a concern Gen. Dan Caine, the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, had raised during weeks of briefings.
“American heroes may be lost and we may have casualties. That often happens in war,” he said in a video statement posted to social media. “We're doing this for the future, and it is a noble mission. We pray for every service member as they selflessly risk their lives to ensure that Americans and our children will never be threatened by a nuclear armed Iran.”

title: Trump Pledges That Iran ‘Will Never Have a Nuclear Weapon’
text:
President Trump said the central aim of the joint U.S. and Israeli attack was to ensure Americans “will never be threatened by a nuclear armed Iran.”
“We sought repeatedly to make a deal,” he said in an eight-minute video posted on Truth Social. “Instead, they attempted to rebuild their nuclear program…They rejected every opportunity to renounce their nuclear ambitions.”
Trump said that after Operation Midnight Hammer last June “obliterated” Iran’s key nuclear sites, he had warned Tehran not to resume its pursuit of nuclear weapons. “Just imagine how emboldened this regime would be if they ever had and actually were armed with nuclear weapons as a means to deliver their message,” he said. There is no evidence of a major reconstitution of Iran’s nuclear program since last June.

title: Iranian State Media Reported Explosions in Several Locations
text:
Use Alt + Down Arrow to expand.
More

title: Trump Offers ‘Complete Immunity’ to Iranian Fighters Who Lay Down Arms
text:
President Trump said any member of Iran’s military or Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps would have “complete immunity” if they laid down their arms.
“I say tonight that you must lay down your weapons and have complete immunity, or, in the alternative, face certain death,” he said in a video message posted to social media.

title: Iran Says Closing Schools Amid U.S. Strikes
text: Schools will be closed and education conducted remotely following the strikes on Iran, state broadcaster IRIB said, citing the education department.

title: Trump Decided to Strike Iran After Briefing on Nuclear Talks
text:
President Trump, after receiving a briefing about nuclear talks Thursday in Geneva, decided that Iran wasn’t serious about negotiations and reached for military options, officials said.
Trump didn’t want to wait roughly a week for further rounds of talks because he didn’t expect Iran to change its mind on ending uranium enrichment, the officials said, deciding by Friday that attacks were the best course of action.

title: Iran Says It Is Preparing to Retaliate
text:
Iran is preparing for retaliation and a response against Israel, according to Iran's Nour News, which is affiliated to the country's Supreme National Security Council.
Tasnim, an Iranian state-news agency said there would be a “crushing response.”

title: Internet Disrupted in Parts of Iran, Watchdog Says
text: Internet connectivity in Iran has dropped to 54% of ordinary levels, according to the digital monitoring group NetBlocks, as the country comes under military strikes from the U.S. and Israel. NetBlocks said live metrics show a disruption in parts of Iran including Tehran.

title: Sirens Sound in Northern Israel
text: Sirens warning of incoming missiles from Iran sounded in northern Israel on Saturday morning. Israelis were instructed to enter safe spaces.

title: Explosions Hit Secure Area Hosting President’s Office, Revolutionary Guards
text: Explosions have taken place in Pasteur Street, a secure area of Tehran that hosts the office of the Iranian president, the center of Iran's Revolutionary Guards intelligence leadership, the Supreme National Security Council and other military institutions. State broadcaster IRIB posted images of explosions and white smoke rising in the area.

title: Bubble of Safety in Wealthy Arab Gulf at Risk of Popping
text: DUBAI—Iran’s Arab Gulf neighbors risk being caught in the fighting, threatening a bubble of security some of them relied on to attract wealthy expatriates from East and West. Dubai, the largest emirate in the hydrocarbon-rich United Arab Emirates, diversified its economy by bringing in multinational corporations from around the world. It sold itself on being a safe place to raise a family, with little crime and relatively insulated from regional conflicts.

title: Trump Vows to Destroy Iran’s Missiles
text:
President Trump said the U.S. had launched a “massive” military operation that would strike Iran’s missile and nuclear programs as well as its navy.
“We are going to destroy their missiles and raze their missile industry to the ground. It will be totally, again, obliterated,” he said. “We are going to annihilate their navy,” he added.

title: Iran's President Not Hurt in Strikes, State Media Says
text: Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian "is in full health and no problem has occurred for him," Tehran's state news agency IRNA said.

title: Trump to Iranians: ‘Take Over Your Government’
text:
President Trump urged Iranians to “take over your government” as the U.S. and Israeli attack continued.
“I say tonight that the hour of your freedom is at hand,” he said in a video posted on Truth Social, warning Iranians to stay at home because “bombs will be dropping everywhere.”

title: Trump Announces Major Combat Operations in Iran
text:
President Trump announced the U.S. military had begun major combat operations against Iran, in a video statement posted early on Saturday.
“Our objective is to defend the American people by eliminating imminent threats from the Iranian regime,” he said, framed by flags and wearing a white USA hat. He blamed Iran for waging “an unending campaign of bloodshed and mass murder” targeting the U.S. and its allies.

title: U.S. Air Defenses Across Middle East Face Test
text:
U.S. air defenses across the Middle East may soon be tested by Iranian missiles if Tehran follows through on its threats to launch a counterattack.
The Pentagon deployed additional Thaad and Patriot anti-missile systems to bases in Jordan, Israel, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia in recent weeks to protect them in the event of a potential conflict. Thaads can intercept ballistic missiles above the Earth’s atmosphere, while Patriots defend against lower-altitude, shorter-range threats. The U.S. also has more than a dozen Navy guided-missile destroyers in the waters of the Middle East and Eastern Mediterranean to take out missiles, drones and other aerial threats.

title: U.S., Israel Plan Days of Heavy Strikes on Iran
text:
The U.S. and Israel plan to carry out several days of intensive attacks on Iran, people familiar with the matter said.
The combined forces are attacking military infrastructure to blunt any Iranian response along with symbols of the regime and targets identified by intelligence, one of the people said.

title: Explosions Heard Across Iran, State Media Says
text:
Iranian state media on Saturday said explosions had been heard across the country amid continuing strikes by the U.S. and Israel.
Explosions were heard in the capital Tehran as well as the cities of Isfahan, Qom, Karaj, Tabriz and Kermanshah and the province of Lorestan, according to state news agency IRNA. Iranian state media said the country’s airspace had been closed for six hours. Tabriz has an airbase and Kermanshah a missile base.

title: Israel Closes Its Airspace
text:
Israeli authorities closed the country’s airspace amid the attack by the U.S. and Israel on Iran. The move came as Israel put the country on heightened alert and banned nonessential public activity as it braced for an Iranian response.
The Civil Aviation Authority told travelers not to go to the airport. It said it would give 24 hours' notice before resuming flights. A spokesman for the agency told official state media that Iran’s airspace would be closed for six hours.

title: Israel Strikes Lebanon Amid Iran Attack
text:
The Israeli military said it struck targets belonging to the militant group Hezbollah in several areas of southern Lebanon on Saturday.
Hezbollah is an important ally of Iran, one of several militia groups throughout the region that Tehran has supported in its long-running shadow war with the U.S. and Israel. Any conflict with Iran risks a wider conflict that could involve those allied militias.

title: U.S. Ambassador to Israel Issues Warning For U.S. Citizens
text:
U.S. Ambassador to Israel Mike Huckabee urged U.S. citizens and embassy staff in the country to prepare and follow Israeli alerts amid the continuing attack on Iran early Saturday.
“We are encouraging all Embassy team & families & US citizens in Israel to be prepared for Home Front Command alerts & sirens,” he posted on X, sharing guidelines from the Israeli military. “Stay near shelters & take immediate action at the sound of alerts or sirens.”
The day before, Huckabee sent an email to embassy staff telling them there was no need to panic but if they wanted to leave Israel, they “should do so TODAY,” according to a U.S. official. It came after previous orders for a partial evacuation of U.S. Embassy personnel in Lebanon on Feb. 23.

title: Explosions in Tehran as Government Symbols Targeted
text:
Iranian state media reported explosions in Tehran on Saturday morning, after the U.S. and Israel launched an attack.
The strikes targeted military assets to blunt Iran’s ability to respond, symbols of the regime and intelligence targets, a person familiar with the matter said. The attack was continuing midmorning local time.

title: War With Iran Threatens the Gulf
text:
U.S. allies in the Gulf, many of which host American military bases and could be vulnerable to Iranian retaliatory strikes, had urged against military action and scrambled to craft a diplomatic solution but fell short.
The new outbreak of war in the region could have substantial economic implications. Iran sits along the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway through which about a fifth of the world’s oil supplies pass, and is just across the Persian Gulf from important Saudi oilfields and the international commercial hub of Dubai.

title: Israel Locks Down the Home Front
text: Sirens rang out in Tel Aviv as Israel announced it had struck Iran, and the government banned all but essential public activities, as the country braced for a retaliatory strike.

title: Live Updates: U.S., Israel Strike Iran; Trump Confirms 'Major Combat Operations'
text:
The U.S. and Israel said they launched an attack on Iran, which retaliated with strikes on Israel and targeted U.S. bases. President Trump confirmed major combat operations against the regime, and urged Iranians to “ take over your government ” as the strikes continued.
“I say tonight that the hour of your freedom is at hand,” Trump said in a video posted on Truth Social. He said the military operation would strike Iran’s missile and nuclear programs as well as its navy. The focus of the strikes, he said, was to ensure Americans “will never be threatened by a nuclear-armed Iran .”
The U.S. and Israel plan to carry out several days of intensive attacks, people familiar with the matter said.
Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said they had launched missile and drone attacks toward Israel.
Tehran is unleashing its stockpile of missiles against all but one of the U.S. bases in the Gulf.
A civilian was killed in the United Arab Emirates by falling debris from intercepted missiles.
Tankers diverted from the Strait of Hormuz and oil prices have been rising over fears of conflict.

title: AI Gave Investors a Glimpse of the Future This Month. And They Sold Their Stocks.
text: Investors have been betting for years that artificial intelligence would change everything. They got some proof of it this month—and then they started selling.

title: Moody’s Puts Paramount on Review for Downgrade to Junk
text:
Moody's Investors Services said Friday it is placing Paramount Global on review for a downgrade following parent company, Paramount Skydance's winning bid to buy to Warner Bros. Discovery. Paramount Global carried Moody's lowest investment grade rating of Baa3 so a downgrade would push the company into speculative grade territory, or "junk" status.
Moody's said that while buying Warner Bros. will improve margins and add revenue diversity for Paramount Skydance, it will also "materially increase" leverage and complexity in the company's debt load. Integrating the two companies will also bring challenges, the ratings firm said.

title: Stocks to Watch Friday Recap: Dell, Duolingo, Compass, CoreWeave
text:
↗️ Dell (DELL): The technology company reported a 39% leap in quarterly sales, boosted by its artificial-intelligence server business. Shares surged 22% Friday.
↘️ Duolingo (DUOL): The educational technology company said it expects a slowdown in bookings and revenue growth this year, as well as lower profitability. Shares skidded 14%.

title: Max Winner — Heard on the Street Recap
text:
Stocks sold off on the last trading day of February. Markets were spooked by a stronger-than-expected reading for producer price inflation. Credit fears also mounted after two business development companies announced dividend cuts. The KBW Bank Index fell 4.9%, Goldman Sachs dropped 7.5% and American Express dived 7.9%. The Dow saw the biggest losses, down 1.1%, or 521 points. The Nasdaq was 0.9% lower and the S&P 500 declined 0.4%.
Oil futures and bonds rallied after the latest round of U.S.-Iran nuclear talks wrapped up without a deal. Front-month Nymex crude rose 2.8% to $67.02. The yield on 10-year Treasuries fell below 4%. Gold futures rose, marking a roughly 11% rally in February, the biggest monthly increase for the precious metal since January 2012.

title: Blackstone’s Schwarzman Took Home $1.2 Billion Last Year
text:
Blackstone CEO Steve Schwarzman collected more than $1.2 billion in dividends and compensation in 2025, according to the firm's annual filing.
The haul, which was in line with Schwarzman's previous record in 2022, came despite lackluster performance for the firm's shares. Including dividends, those lost 7.9%, compared with a 17.9% total return for the S&P 500.

title: Bank Stocks Suffer Biggest Loss Since April
text:
Worries about AI and problems in the private-credit market hit bank stocks on Friday, sending the KBW Nasdaq Bank Index down 4.9% in its worst day since April's tariff turmoil.
Shares of large U.S. lenders slumped across the board, with Goldman Sachs leading the way with a 7.5% decline. Morgan Stanley fell 6.2%. Wells Fargo dropped 5.6%, while Bank of America lost 4.7%.
Payments company Block’s decision to lay off 40% of its staff thanks to AI gains further fueled the belief that technological advancements could lead to broad job losses.
Consumer lenders, more vulnerable during economic recessions, were among the worst performers including American Express, Capital One and Synchrony Financial.

title: Dark Clouds for Solar Stocks This Week
text:
The solar sector’s promising start to 2026 came to a crashing halt this week as a wave of conservative outlooks triggered a selloff. An Invesco ETF tracking solar stocks shed 8% this week, its worst week since last June.
First Solar, which reported earnings Tuesday, projected 2026 revenue of $4.9 billion to $5.2 billion—lower than analysts’ expectation of $6.16 billion. Its stock fell 18% this week.
The solar selloff continued Friday. Sunrun issued a disappointing cash flow outlook for 2026, prompting Jefferies analysts to downgrade the stock. Shares plummeted 35% to finish the week.
The solar industry has faced headwinds in the past year. President Trump's signature megabill, signed into law in July, accelerated the termination of key subsidies for solar projects, and the industry is also heavily exposed to tariffs.
Still, the Invesco ETF is up 12% this year, its rise fueled by AI-related energy demand and reduced regulatory fears.

title: Jobs Data, Earnings From Broadcom and Target: What to Watch Next Week
text:
A heavy slate of economic data will headline the first week of March, with fresh updates on manufacturing and labor. Investors will also weigh tech and retail earnings from the likes of Broadcom and Target. The week culminates in Friday’s highly anticipated February jobs report.
Earnings: Norwegian Cruise Line
Economic data: The Institute for Supply Management will release February's manufacturing purchasing managers index.
Events: Apple's three-day event begins. The technology company is expected to unveil several new products throughout the week.
China: The People’s Bank of China is reducing the risk reserve that banks are required to set aside on certain foreign-exchange contracts. The move, announced Friday, is set to take effect Monday and is viewed as a way for the central bank to combat a recent rally in the yuan.
Courts: The Justice Department's antitrust trial against Live Nation kicks off. The DOJ is seeking a full breakup of the Live Nation-Ticketmaster merger, alleging an illegal monopoly over the live-music industry.

title: Block Stock Rallies After Analysts Praise Earnings and Layoffs
text:
Shares of Block rallied after co-founder Jack Dorsey announced a plan to cut nearly half the payment company's workforce and restructure it for an AI future.
The Square and Cash App owner's stock was up 17% Friday to $63.70.

title: Gold Prices Just Had Their Best Month in More Than a Decade
text:
Gold futures rose roughly 11% in February, the biggest monthly increase for the precious metal since January 2012.
Futures for March delivery ended Friday at $5,230.50 a troy ounce, 1.7% shy of the all-time high set in late January. In dollar-terms, the $516.60 added in February was the most that an ounce of gold has ever gained in a month.

title: Watch: Why Eli Lilly Is Winning the Weight-Loss War
text: WSJ Heard on the Street columnist David Wainer breaks down why Novo Nordisk has struggled and Eli Lilly has prospered in the weight-loss drug market.

title: Heard on the Street: Credit Fears Are Spreading to Consumer Lending
text:
The market’s fears about loans aren’t limited to what’s happening with private credit.
Many headlines on Friday were focused on dividend cuts at business-development companies, which typically lend to privately-held, mid-sized companies.

title: Manufacturing Construction Spending Has Fallen for 11 Straight Months
text:
Spending on U.S. factory construction in December fell for the 11th-straight month, according to new Census Bureau data, the latest sign that President Trump's push to reshore manufacturing is facing pressure.
While factory output has ticked upward in recent months, annualized spending on construction in the sector is now down about 16% from its 2024 high.

title: Shares of Private-Credit Lenders Fall After Dividend Cuts
text: Stocks of the publicly traded private-credit funds have been sliding due to falling interest rates and concerns about their exposure to software companies, which many fear are vulnerable to AI disruption.

title: Economists Expect PCE Inflation to Heat Up
text:
January's hotter-than-expected wholesale-inflation numbers are suggesting that the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation metric may have accelerated last month, too.
The personal-consumption expenditures price index, against which the Federal Reserve measures its 2% inflation target, takes in most of its data from the consumer- and producer-price indexes. So with those numbers in hand, analysts can forecast PCE inflation readings with good accuracy.
Crunching the numbers, many analysts are now projecting that the core PCE price index, which excludes volatile food and energy items, rose by 0.4% or 0.5% last month. Those numbers would bring 12-month core PCE inflation up to 3.1% or 3.2%—the highest reading in more than a year, and even farther from the Fed's target.
Higher PCE inflation would underscore why many Fed officials say they remain focused on addressing rising prices and reluctant to cut interest rates further. The January PCE numbers will arrive on March 13, days before the March 18 Fed decision, which traders widely expect to bring a continued interest-rate pause.

title: Nvidia, Amazon and SoftBank Pledge New OpenAI Funding
text:
That's how much OpenAI was valued at before its latest investment round. The ChatGPT developer secured commitments totaling $110 billion: $50 billion from Amazon and $30 billion each from SoftBank and Nvidia.
Amazon shares were little changed early Friday.
Nvidia dropped roughly 1.5%, extending the chip maker's post-earnings slide.
SoftBank shares closed down 2.6% in Tokyo before the news was out.

title: Spencer Jakab | Warren Buffett and the Giant Gold Cube
text: In the latest edition of my Markets A.M. newsletter, I reflect on Warren Buffett's skepticism toward gold in light of the precious metal's recent trouncing of stocks and fears of dollar debasement.

title: Netflix Stock Rallies After Paramount Wins Warner Bidding War
text: Shares in both Paramount Skydance and Netflix rallied after Paramount prevailed in the bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery.

title: Flutter Stock Falls on Slow NFL Season, Prediction-Markets Threat
text:
Shares of Flutter Entertainment slid after the FanDuel parent reported disappointing revenue and sought to downplay the threat from prediction-market platforms such as Kalshi.
Late Thursday, sports-betting operator Flutter reported revenue of $4.74 billion last quarter, below the $4.93 billion projected by analysts. Adjusted earnings also missed expectations.

title: Treasury Yield Falls Below 4%
text:
U.S. government-bond prices rallied Friday, pushing the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury note below 4% for the first time since November. The move coincided with declines in U.S. stocks and came despite hotter-than-expected wholesale inflation data.
The benchmark yield finished the day at 3.961%, its lowest level since October.

title: Wholesale Inflation Unexpectedly Accelerates
text:
Wholesale inflation unexpectedly sped up in January, the Labor Department said Friday.
The producer-price index increased by 0.5% last month, after rising by 0.4% in December.
Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal had expected a 0.3% increase last month.
Over the 12 months through January, wholesale prices were up 2.9%.
That was down slightly from a month earlier, because large January 2025 price increases dropped out of the 12-month window.

title: Why Block Is Cutting Almost Half Its Staff
text: Jack Dorsey isn’t wasting any time. The chairman and co-founder of Block is cutting 40% of the payments company’s workforce, or more than 4,000 employees, betting artificial-intelligence tools can fill the gap.

title: How AI Cooled Investors’ AI Jitters
text:
Shares in "software as a service" providers have been hit by fears that artificial intelligence would undermine their business models.
Benioff was making the case that software companies can benefit from AI too. That argument got a boost this week when Anthropic featured Salesforce in a presentation about new updates to an AI tool. It also added integration capabilities with other companies' software.

title: Key Twists and Turns in the Warner Deal
text:
Paramount Skydance prevailed over Netflix in the fight for Warner Bros. Discovery. Here’s how it unfolded.
Sept. 11. Warner shares rocket after the Journal reports that Paramount Skydance (recently formed after another merger saga) is preparing a bid. Paramount's move aims to pre-empt a bidding war for Warner's studio-and-streaming unit.
Oct. 21. Warner says it's exploring a sale and reports interest from multiple parties.
Dec. 5. A deal is announced: Netflix will buy Warner (but not its cable networks) for $72 billion.
Dec. 8. Paramount launches a $77.9 billion hostile takeover offer.
Dec. 22. Oracle founder Larry Ellison, father of Paramount chief David Ellison, agrees to backstop Paramount’s offer after Warner calls it “illusory.”
Jan. 12. After being rebuffed by Warner, Paramount says it is planning a proxy fight.
Feb. 17. Warner reopens talks with Paramount, giving it a week for a “best and final” offer.
Feb. 24. Warner says it received an offer of $31 a share from Paramount, up from $30 a share.
Feb. 26. Netflix says it won't match Paramount's offer.

title: China Central Bank Moves to Tame Yuan Rally
text:
China’s central bank on Friday moved to combat a rally in the yuan by making it cheaper for importers to buy dollars and sell the Chinese currency.
The central bank reduced the risk reserve that banks are required to set aside on foreign-exchange forward contracts from 20% to zero. The onshore yuan weakened on Friday morning, declining by about 0.3% against the dollar.

title: Stock Market News, Feb. 27, 2026: Bank Stocks Tumble; Nasdaq Finishes Month With a 3% Loss
text:
Stocks sold off on the last trading day of February, capping a month marked by growing anxiety about the impact of artificial intelligence.
Worries about problems in the private-credit market hit bank stocks, which suffered their worst day since April's tariff turmoil. Goldman Sachs lost 7.5%.
Nvidia shares fell another 4.2%, extending the AI bellwether's post-earnings slide. The chip maker pledged to plow $30 billion into OpenAI's latest funding round, as did SoftBank, while Amazon pledged $50 billion.
The three major gauges all fell Friday. The Dow saw the biggest losses, down 1.1%, or 521 points.
For the month, Nasdaq dropped 3.4%, and the S&P 500 slipped 0.9%, both losses the sharpest since March 2025. The Dow eked out a 0.2% gain, its 10th straight winning month.
Shares of Netflix rallied 14% and Paramount Skydance surged 21% after Paramount won the bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery. Warner shares fell 2.2%.
Wholesale inflation unexpectedly sped up in January, the Labor Department said. Treasury note prices gained, pushing the 10-year yield below 4%. The benchmark yield finished the day at 3.961%, its lowest level since October.
Oil futures rallied after the latest round of U.S.-Iran nuclear talks wrapped up without a deal.

title: Berkshire Earnings, Inflation and Consumer-Confidence Data: Still to Come This Week
text:
Economic data: The producer-price index for January is due at 8:30 a.m. ET, offering more clues on the inflation outlook. The Census Bureau will release data on construction spending for November and December.
Homeland Security shutdown: The partial government closure continues.

title: Nvidia Fails to Reassure — Heard on the Street Thursday Recap
text:
Nvidia’s shares fell 5.5% despite the AI bellwether posting record income. One problem, says columnist Dan Gallagher, is that Nvidia's runaway success could be seen as a sign of destabilization to come.
The Nasdaq composite and S&P 500 declined following Nvidia's slide, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average was little changed and Treasury yields inched lower.

title: Podcast: Nvidia Drags Nasdaq Lower
text: Plus: Zoom Communications stock falls after missing expectations, while J.M. Smucker rallies on agreement with activist investor.

title: Block, Netflix, Paramount, Dell: Stocks to Watch in Afterhours Trading
text:
↗️ Block (XYZ): The payments company founded by Jack Dorsey that includes Square and Cash App said Thursday that it plans to lay off 40% of its workforce, or more than 4,000 employees. Shares surged more than 20% in postmarket trading.
↗️ Dell Technologies (DELL): The technology company's stock jumped 10% in afterhours trading, following a 39% increase in sales, driven in part by growth in Dell's AI server business.

title: What Nvidia’s Slide Today Shows About the Stock Market
text:
Some investors said the slide in Nvidia stock Thursday shows how, for AI-linked companies, the bar to please investors—and assuage bubble worries —keeps getting higher.
Technology stocks have borne the brunt of investors’ skepticism, as concerns mount about stretched valuations and AI’s impact on corporate earnings. The S&P 500’s information-technology sector is on track for its fourth straight monthly decline, its longest losing streak since 2018, according to Dow Jones Market Data.
“With higher valuations, obviously there’s a lot more pressure on current earnings,” said Chris Zaccarelli, chief investment officer for Northlight Asset Management. He attributed the dynamic to investors’ looking past results and toward how AI disruption could squeeze earnings in the years ahead.
Bob Elliott, chief executive of Unlimited Funds, wrote in a Thursday note that Nvidia was an example of companies that are “growing into their valuations.” At this pace, "it won’t take long for U.S. equities to grow into their current valuations, resetting them to near pre-covid levels,” he wrote.

title: Accenture Stock Jumps After Firm Partners With Mistral in European AI Push
text:
Accenture said Thursday it struck a multi-year deal with French startup Mistral AI to help enterprise clients deploy artificial-intelligence technology, with a particular focus on European companies seeking alternatives to U.S.-based providers.
Accenture stock surged 8.3%, its largest percent increase since April 2020.
Despite today's move, shares of the professional-services company are down sharply in 2026, nearly 23%, and on track for its worst month on record, according to Dow Jones Market Data.
As part of the deal, Accenture will become a Mistral customer itself, deploying the startup's models across its roughly 784,000-person workforce.

title: Nvidia Fails to Reassure—Heard on the Street Recap
text: Nvidia’s shares fell 5.5% despite record income as investors' high expectations for AI bellwethers continue to create a difficult bar for companies to clear. One particular problem, notes Heard columnist Dan Gallagher, is that the company’s runaway success could be seen as a sign of the destabilization to come, given the massive amounts of capital-spending filling its coffers are financially weakening the world’s largest companies from Amazon to Microsoft.

title: Natural-Gas Price Spike Could Cost International Paper More Than $40 Million
text: International Paper, which was plagued by gas curtailments at its plants in Texas and surging prices at other containerboard mills and box plants, warned investors during the storm that the bad weather could cost it $20 million to $25 million. "It will definitely be bigger than that because of natural gas," Chief Executive Andy Silvernail said at an investor conference today. "Probably in the $40 million to $50 million range is my guess."

title: Nvidia Stock Slides 5.5%, Biggest Drop in 10 Months
text:
Shares in Nvidia skidded Thursday, despite the huge chip maker posting a jump in profit and record quarterly sales.
The stock fell 5.5%, a move that erased $259 billion in market cap from the company, according to Dow Jones Market Data.
The decline in market value was the seventh largest for a public company on record, and the fifth largest for Nvidia. Thursday's fall also marked Nvidia's biggest one-day drop since April, a period marked by flaring trade tensions.
Nvidia is still the world's largest company by market cap. In October, it became the first $5 trillion company, a milestone it later dropped back below.
Asked on CNBC Thursday about the company's valuation being lower than some other chip companies, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang responded: "The market can't hold us back forever, and the reason for that is because we're going to keep on growing."

title: Trump Brothers’ Bitcoin Miner Posts Quarterly Loss
text:
American Bitcoin, the bitcoin mining and treasury firm backed by Eric Trump and Donald Trump Jr., swung to a loss last quarter.
The company, formed in March with its spinoff from another bitcoin miner, reported a net loss of $59 million. In the previous quarter, it had earned $3.5 million.
Revenue rose to $78 million in the three months ended December, from $64 million in the third quarter.
For the year, the company lost $153 million on revenue of $185 million. The loss was primarily driven by a mark-to-market loss on its bitcoin holdings.
American Bitcoin debuted as a publicly traded company after a reverse merger in September. Shares have plummeted since then. They were up about 3% Thursday.
The company ended 2025 with 5,401 bitcoins.

title: When AI Jitters Hit Software Stocks
text:
The $1.6 Trillion Meltdown That Swept Through Software Stocks
Concern over the threat AI poses has hit the shares of companies like Salesforce and Adobe hard.

title: Stocks to Watch Thursday Recap: Zoom, Trade Desk, Warner, Paramount
text:
🔎 Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD): The entertainment company said Paramount Skydance's (PSKY) revised bid to buy it could reasonably lead to a better proposal than its agreement with Netflix (NFLX). Netflix stock gained 2.3%, while Paramount jumped 10%. Warner shares edged lower 0.4%.
↘️ Zoom Communications (ZM): The video-conferencing platform's adjusted earnings per-share disappointed, as did its guidance. Shares dropped 12% Thursday.

title: AI Will Transform Every Industry, Nvidia CEO Says
text: Agentic systems represent a third inflection point for artificial intelligence with the potential to revolutionize the economy, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said in a CNBC interview Thursday morning. “The number of industries that are affected by AI, because AI is fundamentally a new way of doing software, is going to be very broad based,” Huang said. “Every single company's affected, every industry will be transformed, and every country will power it.”

title: Paramount Stock Jumps After Earnings, as Warner Weighs Its Offer
text:
Shares in Paramount Skydance rallied about 10% after the entertainment and media company—which is pursuing a bid for rival media company Warner Bros. Discovery— reported earnings. Here's what to know:
Revenue for the direct-to-consumer segment, which includes the Paramount+ streaming service, rose 10% in the fourth quarter. Its TV segment was weaker, with Paramount flagging more difficulty ahead as pay-TV subscribers decline.
CEO David Ellison said the company views a deal with Warner as “an accelerant” to achieving its goals more quickly. Earlier this week, Paramount revised its bid for Warner to $31 a share.
Warner reiterated on Thursday that Paramount’s latest offer could open the door to beating its signed agreement with Netflix.

title: ‘Monroe Doctrine Extreme’ and Other Wild-Card Market Risks
text:
Shares of private-credit firms have been sinking as investors fret about what artificial intelligence means for the software companies they have lent money to.
Analysts at Citi include the possibility of AI disruption causing distress in credit markets in a list of market wild cards—risks that probably won't materialize but just might. Here's a selection of the others.

text: That's the number of companies that have filed lawsuits seeking refunds on President Trump's tariffs, many of which the Supreme Court has struck down.

title: Salesforce Stock Gains After Earnings, AI Reassurance
text:
Salesforce shares rose Thursday after the software maker said it expects revenue growth to be stable and sought to reassure investors it can stay relevant in the artificial-intelligence age.
Salesforce's quarterly results cleared expectations, with revenue growing 12%.
This fiscal year, the company expects its top line to rise by about 10%, in line with last year.
Chief Executive Marc Benioff said he wasn't fazed by the recent selloff in software stocks. “This is not our first SaaS-pocalypse,” he said, referring to software-as-a-service.
Shares (CRM) closed 4% higher. Through Thursday, the stock had lost about one-quarter of its value in 2026.

title: Nvidia Earnings: By the Numbers
text:
AI chip giant Nvidia reported a leap in income and record quarterly sales . Here are the key figures:
Fourth-quarter sales came in at $68.1 billion, beating the $66.1 billion expected by analysts. In November, Nvidia raised its sales guidance for the quarter to $65 billion.
Revenue in the all-important data center division surged 75% to $62.3 billion.
Net income was $43 billion, up 94% from a year earlier and trumping analysts' expectations.
Nvidia expects $78 billion in revenue this quarter, significantly higher than Wall Street had predicted, and gross margins of 75%, slightly up on forecasts.
However, the stock fell about 3% Thursday morning.

title: Nvidia Earnings: How Does the Stock React on Average?
text:
Nvidia's stock fell early Thursday, despite the $4 trillion-plus chip maker posting a near-doubling in quarterly profit and record sales .
Shares were down roughly 3% in recent trading.
Although the semiconductor designer has met or topped Wall Street’s targets for at least five years, its earnings have triggered diverging stock moves:
Over the last five years, the stock has averaged a gain of 2.5% the day after earnings, according to Dow Jones Market Data.
But on several occasions, shares dropped more than 3%, a sign of just how high market hopes were.

title: Cold Is Winning the Coffee Wars at Starbucks
text:
Starbucks’s business is going cold. Around two-thirds of the chain’s U.S. orders are now iced, up from 37% in 2013.
The trend extends beyond the U.S., with 60% of the chain’s global sales represented by cold espressos, fruity Refreshers, iced matcha and chilled tea lattes.

title: Weekly Jobless Claims Rise Less Than Expected
text:
That's the number of people who filed for unemployment benefits in the week through Feb. 21.
It is up from 208,000 a week earlier, but below the 215,000 claims that economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal expected.

title: David Wainer | Shrinking Middle Class Calls for Big Food Rethink
text:
A combination of stubborn food inflation and record income inequality is forcing global food majors to rethink how they market to different economic strata.
In the latest edition of my Markets A.M. newsletter, I look at how depressed valuations in the food sector may offer an opportunity for investors—provided companies can successfully adapt their playbook.

title: Here’s What Wall Street Is Saying About Nvidia’s Record Quarter
text:
Stock analysts raced to lift forecasts for Nvidia's sales and earnings growth after the AI chipmaker posted a 94% rise in quarterly profit and record revenue on Wednesday. Here's what Wall Street is saying:
Dan Ives of Wedbush, a staunch AI optimist, described the results as "like watching Michael Jordan in his first few years for the Chicago Bulls." Ives said the results show demand for Nvidia's chips is even stronger than anticipated. The company could reach a market value of $6 trillion by next year, he said. It was worth $4.8 trillion as of Wednesday's close.
JPMorgan raised its price target on the stock to $265 a share, and said worries that the likes of Meta and Amazon won't be able to keep up the pace of AI investment are overblown. Nvidia's closing price Wednesday was $195.56.
Christopher Rolland's team at Susquehanna International said Nvidia overdelivered in the quarter, and that they now expect the company to ramp up spending. Susquehanna expects Nvidia's operating expenses to nearly double to $30 billion in the fiscal year ending early 2027 while keeping margins stable.
John Vinh at KeyBanc Capital Markets had expected even stronger data-center revenue from Nvidia this quarter, but still described the results as encouraging. KeyBanc now expects Nvidia's annual revenues could reach almost $500 billion by 2028.
Gil Luria at D.A. Davidson said the results show no sign demand for chips is faltering. "We are simply not seeing demand slowing down for compute for the foreseeable future," he wrote.
Cody Acree at Benchmark pointed to the relatively muted market reaction, saying investors have "largely come to view these types of stellar reports as more the norm, rather than the standout results that they were."

title: For Nvidia, Quelling AI Jitters Is a Tall Order — Heard on the Street
text:
Nvidia's free cash flow stood at $96.7 billion at the end of its fiscal year, and is seen swelling further .
That's great for investors for now. But the chip designer's runaway success could be seen as a sign of future destabilization: Massive capital spending is filling Nvidia's coffers while financially weakening the largest companies and employers.
